did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically Basic CNN, VGG16, and DenseNet201, to classify skin diseases using the ISIC dataset. The study involved training these deep learning models and evaluating their performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from implementing and comparing three CNN architectures (Basic CNN, VGG16, DenseNet201) on the ISIC dataset. It includes specific methodology, training procedures, and quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) in Table I, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: AI-Powered Skin Disease Recognition: An Implementation of CNN Architectures
journal: 2025 International Conference on Electronics and Computing, Communication Networking Automation Technologies (ICEC2NT)
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of three Convolutional Neural Network architectures—Basic CNN, VGG16, and DenseNet201—for automated skin disease classification using the ISIC dataset. The models were trained and assessed using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score to identify the most suitable architecture for dermatological applications. The experimental results indicated that VGG16 outperformed the other models in terms of overall diagnostic accuracy and precision. Although DenseNet201 was expected to excel due to its dense connections, it achieved a lower classification accuracy of less than 0.9041 compared to VGG16. The findings suggest that VGG16 is a more appropriate choice for AI-assisted skin disease diagnosis among the tested architectures. The study highlights the potential of CNN-based models to support early diagnosis while noting challenges such as data imbalance and image variability.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (e.g., melanoma, actinic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is 'skin disease classification' using CNNs. The model classifies input images into discrete diagnostic categories. Specifically, the text mentions classifying a skin lesion 'into one of seven possible classifications' using a Softmax activation function to assign probability scores to each class and selecting the class with the highest probability. The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions as well as specific disease types.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A widely regarded standard reference for dermatological research, used as the primary benchmark for this study. It contains labeled images of various skin diseases including melanoma, actinic keratosis, and squamous cell carcinoma.

use.validation: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).
use.other: Served as the primary benchmark dataset for the study.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 10,015 dermatoscopic pictures of pigmented skin lesions, gathered from hospital and dermatological clinic settings and annotated by certified dermatologists. It includes seven types of common skin diseases with variations in morphology, age, and skin type.

use.training: Not used for training in this study; mentioned in the context of literature survey and image acquisition methodology description.

use.other: Described as a recognized benchmark in dermatology research and used to describe the image acquisition process and class distribution characteristics relevant to the study's context.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study compared model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score without statistical significance tests.

ISIC Dataset.notes: VGG16 outperformed the other models in terms of overall diagnostic accuracy and precision. DenseNet201 achieved a lower classification accuracy compared to VGG16. The study notes that DenseNet201 had over 20-fold shorter training time than VGG16.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; VGG16 is described as a 'popular convolutional neural network' and 'enhanced version' used as a fundamental building block, but specific pretraining weights (e.g., ImageNet) are not detailed.
architecture_used.input_size: Resized to a standard dimension (exact pixel dimensions not reported)

architecture_details.Basic CNN: Simple Convolutional Neural Network with convolutional layers for feature extraction (texture, color, lesion boundaries).
architecture_details.VGG16: Deep CNN with sequential stacking of convolutional layers for hierarchical feature extraction. Described as an 'enhanced version' of the standard VGG-16 architecture.
architecture_details.DenseNet201: Deep CNN with dense connections across all layer types to facilitate gradient propagation and efficient feature utilization, reducing computational redundancy.

training_details.regularization: Batch normalization used to accelerate convergence and stabilize learning; data augmentation (rotation, flipping, contrast adjustment, normalization) used to minimize overfitting.
training_details.training_time_note: DenseNet201 had over 20-fold shorter training time than VGG16.

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Class with the highest probability score

Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, and contrast adjustment

conclusions: VGG16 outperformed the other tested architectures (Basic CNN and DenseNet201) in terms of overall diagnostic accuracy (88%) and precision (0.89) for skin disease classification.
DenseNet201 achieved a classification accuracy of 71%, which was lower than VGG16 but higher than the Basic CNN (66%), while offering over 20-fold shorter training time compared to VGG16.
The Basic CNN model demonstrated lower performance with an accuracy of 66% and precision/recall/F1-scores of approximately 0.65.

CNN-based models serve as effective decision-support tools for early diagnosis and timely treatment of skin diseases, potentially mitigating issues related to the subjectivity and limited accessibility of manual dermatological diagnosis.
The study acknowledges challenges in skin disease classification, including varying disease appearances, similarities across disease classes, and differences in image acquisition circumstances.
Data imbalance and image variability were noted as limitations affecting model generalization and performance.
Future work aims to improve model efficiency for mobile applications, systemize interpretations, and explore complex decision-making suggestions for real-time clinical settings.
important_remarks: Contradictory findings regarding model performance: The abstract states DenseNet201 outperformed other architectures with the highest accuracy, while the results section and conclusions state VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy (88%) and precision (0.89), with DenseNet201 at 71%.
Discrepancy in model count: The abstract mentions 'Four different CNN architectures' were utilized, but only three (Basic CNN, VGG16, DenseNet201) are detailed in the methodology and results.

Lack of quantitative detail on data imbalance: The paper acknowledges data imbalance as a limitation causing bias toward majority classes but does not provide specific class distribution statistics or mitigation strategies beyond general augmentation.
No external validation: The study relies solely on the ISIC dataset without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability claims.

Missing statistical significance testing: The comparison of model performances is based on point estimates of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score without reported p-values or confidence intervals to establish statistical significance.
Unreported hyperparameters: Key training details such as the exact number of epochs, learning rate, batch size, and specific data augmentation parameters are not explicitly reported.

Ambiguous dataset description: The paper references both ISIC and HAM10000 datasets in the literature survey and methodology context but does not clearly specify if the primary experiments were conducted on ISIC, HAM10000, or a combined subset, creating ambiguity about the data source.

Limited clinical relevance assessment: The study focuses on technical metrics (accuracy, precision) and does not assess clinical utility, such as sensitivity for melanoma detection specifically or false-negative rates in a clinical context.
